qas.a:
Airways
Bones/soft tissue
Cardiac silhouette/mediastinum
Diaphragm and pleura
Everything else (effusions)
Fields (lung fields)

qas.q: Right and left markers for extremities are indicate...
qas.a: which side of the body the xray was taken

qas.q: How do vessles and bronchi show up on chest xray?
qas.a: White lines

qas.q: Are bronchi normally visible on chest xray?
qas.a: No - they're filled with air and have thin walls

qas.q: Can you normally see the visceral/parietal pleura on a chest xray?
qas.a: No

qas.q: Is there normally air in the pleural space?
qas.a: No

qas.q: In an upright chest xray, blood flow to the bases is (greater/lesser) than at the apices
qas.a: greater - due to gravity

qas.q: Vessel lumen size is generally (bigger/smaller) at the bases of lungs than at the apices
qas.a: bigger

"Ryan Has Found The Diaphragm"
The retrosternal space
Hilar region
Fissures
Thoracic spine
Diaphragm and posterior costophrenic sulci
Lucent crescent between sternum and ascending aorta
no discrete mass present
Major and minor fissures should be pencil point thin, if even seen
Rectangular vertebral bodies with parallel end plates, disk spacecs maintain height

qas.q: Normal Diaphragm and posterior costophrenic sulci
qas.a: Right hemidiaphragm slightly higher than the left, sharp posterior costophrenic sulci

qas.q: What do the costophrenic angles normally look like?
qas.a: Acute and sharply outlined
pleural effusion

qas.q: How much pleural fluid is required to cause a appreciable blunting of the posterior costophrenic angle on the lateral view?
qas.a: 100 mL

qas.q: How much pleural fluid is required to produce a blunting of the costophrenic angle on a PA view Chest Xray?
qas.a: 300 mL
<50% - heart is less than 1/2 of internal diameter of thoracic cage

qas.q: Airspace (alveolar) disease on a chest xray looks like...
qas.a:
Opacities described as fluffy, cloudlike, and hazy
Bronchograms may be present
Series of black, branching tubular structures - sign of airspace disease

qas.q: Interstitial disease presents as what on a chest xray?
qas.a:
Discrete, reticular, nodular, or reticulonodular patterns
"Pockets" of disesae are separated by normal appearing aerated lung

qas.q: what does a spine sign on a lateral x-ray aid in identifying
qas.a: Lower lobe process such as pneumonia
Two substances of the same density touch each other and blur the edges between them - can't tell where one object begins and other ends

qas.q: Silhouette and spine sign aid in localizing...
qas.a: pneumonia

Discrete reticular, nodular, or reticulonodular patterns
"Packets" of disease are separated by normal aerated lung
No air bronchograms present

Connective tissue
Lymphatics
Blood vessels
Bronchi
Lots of lines
Lots of dots
Lines and dots
Thickening of bronchovascular/axial interstitium - sign of pulmonary edema
Thickening of the peripheral interstitium near the costophrenic angle - sign of pulmonary edema
Cavitary lesion in upper lobe is pathogneumonic for TB
Cavitary lesion in upper lobe

qas.q: What X ray finding is pathogneumonic for TB?
qas.a: 1. Atelectasis of the entire lung - complete obstruction of L or R bronchus

air
Opaque
Shift of the heart and trachea towards side of atelectasis

qas.q: Atelactasisis the abscence of ____ in the lung. So the hemithorax will look more (opaque/black) than normal
qas.a:
Visible in airspace disease
Visibility of air in bronchus because of surrounding airspace disease

qas.q: Chest X ray signs of atelectasis
qas.a:
compressing
push
away from the side of opacification
opaque - since it no longer contains air
pneumonia

qas.q: With a large pleural effusion, fluid such as blood, exudate/transudate can fill the pleural space and act like a mass ______ the underyling tissue and _____ the trachea/heart ______
qas.a:
firbous tissue
opaque
toward side of opacification

Complete or partial collapse of a lung or lobe of a lung, alveoli become deflated
Appears white when fluid/soft tissue is substituted for air or when air is reabsorbed
No new air can enter the portion of lung distal to collapse/obstruction

qas.q: If you see an opacified hemithorax, think ____
qas.a:
1. Displacement of the major/minor fissure toward the atelectasis
2. Increased density of the atelectatic portion of the lung
3. Shift of the mobile structures in the throax (heart, trachea, hemidiaphragms)
4. Compensatory overinflation of the unaffected segments, lobes or lungs

qas.q: After a pneumonectomy, _____ fills the hemithroax resulting in a ____ appearance and a heart/trachea shift ____
qas.a:
1. Mucus plug
2. Tumors
3. Foreign body aspiration
4. inflammation
Build up of excess fluid between the visceral and parietal pleura
Blunting of the posterior costophrenic sulcus (lateral view)

qas.q: Most common causes of obstructive atelectasis
qas.a: blunting of the lateral costophrenic sulcus (frontal view)
Opacification of the entire hemithorax in an adult

qas.q: 75-100 ml of fluid in pleural space shows...
qas.a: fluffy, indistinct, usually homogenous in density

qas.q: 200-300 ml of fluid in pleural space shows....
qas.a: will cause prominence of the interstitial tissues (areas outside of the airspace) of the lung in the affected area

qas.q: 2000 ml (2 liters) of fluid in pleural space shows...
qas.a:
Lobar
Segmental
Interstitial
Round
Cavitary
Homogenous consolidation of affected lung with air bronchogram
Patchy airspace disease, no air bronchogram, atelectasis can be present
Most often in right lower lobe

1. Parietal pleura remains adjacent to the inner surface of the chest wall, but the visceral pleura retracts toward hilum w/ collapsed lung
2. Visceral pleura becomes a visible thin white line
Visceral pleural line

qas.q: Right middle lobe pneumonia with air bronchogram
qas.a: visceral pleural line
Leakage of air in the pleural space, and there is no shift in mediastinal structures

Progressively large amount of air gets trapped in the potential space between parietal and visceral layers
Occurs when there's a one-way valve allowing air into, but not out of, the pleural space
shift in mediastinal structures to opposite side
CANNOT MISS

qas.q: In order to dx a pneumothorax, you must be able to identify...
qas.a:
Bullae
Skinfolds
Medial border of scapula

qas.q: Usually in a pneumothorax, there is an absence of lung markings peripheral to the....
qas.a: Subcutaneous air on x ray
3-5cm
Right main or right lower lobe bronchi bc it's the path of least resistance
chronic inflammatory disease in which small nodules (granulomas) develop in lungs, lymph nodes, and other organs

Hyperinflation of lungs
Dark lung fields
Low set diaphragm in 11th or 12th posterior ribs
Heart is vertical and narrow
Flattened diaphragms in lateral chest
Retrosternal air is increased
Increased AP diameter

qas.q: The tip of an endotracheal tube should be about _____ cm above the carina
qas.a:
1. Kerley B lines (usually at lung bases near the costophrenic angle. Kerley A lines extend from the hila but do not reach the periphery of lung
2. Peribronchial cuffing (interstitial fluid accumulates around bronchi)
3. Fluid in fissures
4. Pleural effusion

qas.q: Where are endotracheal tubes most commonly malpositioned?
qas.a:
1. Fluffy, indistinct airspace disease
2. Batwing configuration
3. Pleural effusion if edema is cardiogenic
Air in the pleural space separates the visceral from parietal pleura
The pneumothorax itself will appear "black" (air density). The hemithorax may appear more lucent than normal

qas.q: Key findings in pulmonary interstitial edema
qas.a: There is NEVER a shift of theheart or trachea toward TOWARD the side of a pneumothorax

qas.q: Key findings in pulmonary alveolar edema
qas.a: Visceral and parietal pleura do not separate
Atelectasis is the absence of air in the lung. The hemithorax will appear more oqauque
There is almost always a shift of the heart and trachea TOWARD the side of atelectasis
Left hemidiaphragm moves upward and may disappear (silhouette sign)
contact between the parietal pleura and visceral pleura is disruted

qas.q: Density changes in an obstructive atelectasis
qas.a:
Pleural effusion
Pneumothrorax
The increased pressure on the great vessels causes a decrease in venous return, reduced CO, decreased BP, hyoxia, and eventual shock
TB
bronchograms

qas.q: The two most common caues/etiologies of a non-obstructive atelectasis are...
qas.a:
asthma
emphysema
chornic bronchitis (COPD)
there's an obstruction of an airway

qas.q: Unilateral hilar adenopathy may be the only manifestation of primary infection of TB
qas.a:
Pleural effusion
Pneumothorax

qas.q: cavitary lesion in the upper lobe is pretty pathogneumonic for....
qas.a: airspace disease
